title: After Sunset - Sonnets
content:
В В В В пїЅSi quis piorum Manibus locus.пїЅ


I.

В В В В Straight from the sunпїЅs grave in the deep clear west
В В В В A sweet strong wind blows, glad of life: and I,
В В В В Under the soft keen stardawn whence the sky
В В В В Takes life renewed, and all nightпїЅs godlike breast
В В В В Palpitates, gradually revealed at rest
В В В В By growth and change of ardours felt on high,
В В В В Make onward, till the last flame fall and die
В В В В And all the world by nightпїЅs broad hand lie blest.
В В В В Haply, meseems, as from that edge of death,
В В В В Whereon the day lies dark, a brightening breath
В В В В Blows more of benediction than the morn,
В В В В So from the graves whereon grief gazing saith
В В В В That half our heart of life there lies forlorn
В В В В May light or breath at least of hope be born.



II.

В В В В The wind was soft before the sunset fled:
В В В В Now, while the cloud-enshrouded corpse of day
В В В В Is lowered along a red funereal way
В В В В Down to the dark that knows not white from red,
В В В В A clear sheer breeze against the night makes head,
В В В В Serene, but sure of life as ere a ray
В В В В Springs, or the dusk of dawn knows red from grey,
В В В В Being as a soul that knows not quick from dead.
В В В В From far beyond the sunset, far above,
В В В В Full toward the starry soundless east it blows
В В В В Bright as a childпїЅs breath breathing on a rose,
В В В В Smooth to the sense as plume of any dove;
В В В В Till more and more as darkness grows and glows
В В В В Silence and night seem likest life and love.



III.

В В В В If light of life outlive the set of sun
В В В В That men call death and end of all things, then
В В В В How should not that which life held best for men
В В В В And proved most precious, though it seem undone
В В В В By force of death and woful victory won,
В В В В Be first and surest of revival, when
В В В В Death shall bow down to life arisen again?
В В В В So shall the soul seen be the self-same one
В В В В That looked and spake with even such lips and eyes
В В В В As love shall doubt not then to recognise,
В В В В And all bright thoughts and smiles of all time past
В В В В Revive, transfigured, but in spirit and sense
В В В В None other than we knew, for evidence
В В В В That loveпїЅs last mortal word was not his last
meaning:
Exploring the themes of classic, Algernon Charles Swinburne delivers a powerful performance in "After Sunset - Sonnets"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
